findings.title: Undeclared network usage
findings.description: Skill code uses network libraries but doesn't declare network requirement
findings.remediation: Declare network usage in compatibility field or remove network calls

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The skill uses network-dependent commands and a Python script that fetches remote content, but the manifest does not explicitly declare network requirements. This is a policy/metadata issue rather than a direct exploit.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Low; it is not an attack by itself, but it can hide external dependency behavior from reviewers.
metadata.meta_impact: Reduced transparency and potential tool-policy mismatch; may enable unnoticed external fetches.

findings.title: Credential and secret extraction guidance
findings.description: The instructions include multiple workflows for locating and dumping secrets, tokens, and credentials from cloud environments, including Azure Key Vault secrets, VM userdata, Lambda environment variables, metadata service credentials, and stored gcloud credentials. This is direct data-exposure guidance that can be used for unauthorized access.
findings.snippet: "Dump Key Vault secrets"; "Extract VM UserData"; "Extract Lambda environment variables (may contain secrets)"; "Access metadata service"; "Find stored credentials"
findings.remediation: Remove secret-harvesting steps, restrict the skill to validating exposure without revealing secret values, and require explicit authorization plus redaction of sensitive outputs.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The instructions explicitly describe dumping Key Vault secrets, VM userdata, Lambda environment variables, metadata credentials, and stored gcloud credentials. This is direct secret-extraction guidance.
metadata.meta_exploitability: High; the steps are immediately actionable against exposed or misconfigured cloud assets.
metadata.meta_impact: Credential theft, unauthorized access, and potential lateral movement across cloud environments.

findings.title: Indirect prompt injection / transitive trust via referenced external content
findings.description: The skill references additional cloud scripts and advanced materials that are not present in the package. If these referenced files are fetched from external or user-controlled sources, they could inject malicious instructions or unsafe procedures into the agent workflow.
findings.snippet: "References: [Advanced Cloud Scripts] - Azure Automation runbooks, Function Apps enumeration, AWS data exfiltration, GCP advanced exploitation" and missing referenced files
findings.remediation: Bundle all referenced materials locally, avoid external instruction sources, and validate any imported content as untrusted before use.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The skill references additional cloud scripts and advanced materials that are not present, creating a transitive-trust risk if those files are sourced externally or from untrusted locations.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Medium; exploitation depends on how referenced content is obtained, but the trust boundary is clearly unsafe.
metadata.meta_impact: Indirect prompt injection or unsafe instruction adoption from external referenced content.

findings.title: Capability inflation and deceptive cloud-penetration positioning
findings.description: The skill is framed as a general-purpose cloud security assessment tool, but its instructions explicitly include offensive actions such as credential bypass, persistence, secret extraction, and exploitation of misconfigurations. The naming and trigger phrases also aggressively broaden activation to sensitive cloud-targeting tasks, increasing the chance of misuse beyond legitimate assessment.
findings.snippet: "perform cloud penetration testing", "exploit cloud misconfigurations", "extract secrets from cloud environments"; sections on "Azure Exploitation", "Azure Persistence", "AWS Exploitation", "AWS Persistence"
findings.remediation: Tighten the description to authorized defensive assessment only, remove offensive/persistence language, and clearly scope the skill to safe auditing and reporting actions.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The skill is explicitly framed around offensive cloud penetration tasks and includes persistence, exploitation, and secret extraction workflows, which is capability inflation beyond benign assistance.
metadata.meta_exploitability: High; the skill is designed to be invoked for sensitive cloud-targeting tasks.
metadata.meta_impact: Misuse of the skill for offensive cloud operations and unauthorized assessment behavior.

findings.title: Unauthorized tool and persistence abuse
findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to import stolen contexts, export access tokens for persistence, create backdoor service principals, add admin roles, and create new access keys. These are tool-abuse and persistence behaviors that exceed normal assessment and can compromise cloud accounts.
findings.snippet: "Import-AzContext -Profile 'C:\Temp\StolenToken.json'"; "Save-AzContext -Path C:\Temp\AzureAccessToken.json"; "Create backdoor service principal"; "Create backdoor access key"
findings.remediation: Eliminate persistence and stolen-context workflows, prohibit creation of new privileged identities/keys, and limit actions to read-only verification and reporting.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The instructions include importing stolen contexts, exporting access tokens, creating backdoor service principals, and creating new access keys. These are concrete persistence and tool-abuse actions.
metadata.meta_exploitability: High; the commands are directly usable in cloud environments with sufficient privileges.
metadata.meta_impact: Account compromise, persistence, privilege escalation, and unauthorized access retention.

findings.title: Potential compute exhaustion through unbounded cloud enumeration and spraying guidance
findings.description: The workflow encourages broad enumeration across multiple cloud providers, repeated region-wide scans, and password spraying with IP rotation. These patterns can create excessive load, trigger rate limits, and degrade availability of target services or the agent environment.
findings.snippet: "Use slow, deliberate enumeration"; "spread across regions"; "Use multiple credentials"; examples using loops over all buckets/regions
findings.remediation: Add strict rate limits, bounded scopes, and explicit user confirmation for any repeated or large-scale enumeration.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The workflow encourages broad enumeration, repeated region-wide scans, and password spraying guidance. While not an infinite loop, it can create excessive load and trigger rate limits.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Medium; impact depends on scale and target throttling.
metadata.meta_impact: Service degradation, alerting, and potential denial-of-service-like pressure on cloud APIs.

findings.title: Vague skill description
findings.description: Skill description is too short (16 chars). Provide detailed explanation.
findings.remediation: Provide a clear, detailed description of what the skill does and when to use it

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: This is a metadata quality warning about a short description, not a security issue in the code or instructions.

findings.title: Skill name does not follow agent skills naming rules
findings.description: Skill name 'Cloud Penetration Testing__CI_B8' is invalid. Agent skills require lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens only, with a maximum length of 64 characters.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The finding concerns naming convention compliance, which is a packaging/policy issue rather than a security threat.
metadata.meta_impact: Potential skill registry or validation failure.

findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
findings.description: Skill manifest does not include a 'license' field. Specifying a license helps users understand usage terms.
findings.remediation: Add 'license' field to SKILL.md frontmatter (e.g., MIT, Apache-2.0)

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: Missing license metadata is a documentation/policy issue, not a security vulnerability.
metadata.meta_impact: Unclear usage terms and possible compliance friction.

findings.title: Dangerous data flow in command pipeline
findings.description: Pipeline downloads data from the network and executes it: `curl https://sdk.cloud.google.com | bash`. This is a remote code execution pattern.
findings.remediation: Review the command pipeline. Avoid piping sensitive data to network commands or shell execution.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The code literally pipes a remote download into bash (`curl https://sdk.cloud.google.com | bash`), which is a classic remote code execution pattern.
metadata.meta_exploitability: High; any compromise of the remote source or network path can execute arbitrary code.
metadata.meta_impact: Arbitrary code execution during installation or setup.

findings.title: Dangerous data flow in command pipeline
findings.description: Pipeline reads data and passes it to code execution: `cat ips.txt | python3 ip2provider.py`. Review for potential command injection.
findings.remediation: Review the command pipeline. Avoid piping sensitive data to network commands or shell execution.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: This is a generic pipeline warning on `cat ips.txt | python3 ip2provider.py`; the snippet is a normal local file-to-script pipeline and no injection sink is shown.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Low; no evidence of unsafe command construction in the provided context.

findings.title: SKILL DISCOVERY ABUSE detected by YARA
findings.description: Detects protocol manipulation via capability inflation in skill discovery: backdoor access
findings.remediation: Review and remove skill discovery abuse pattern

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The YARA hit is backed by the actual instruction text about creating a backdoor access key, which is clearly malicious persistence guidance.
metadata.meta_exploitability: High; the instruction is directly actionable in AWS environments.
metadata.meta_impact: Unauthorized persistence and long-term account compromise.

findings.title: Arbitrary command execution via downloaded binary execution
findings.description: The Python script downloads a remote binary and executes it with os.system. This creates a direct command-execution path controlled by external content and can run arbitrary code on the host if the remote payload is replaced or compromised.
findings.remediation: Remove remote binary execution entirely. If optimization is needed, use a vetted local library or signed, pinned artifacts with integrity verification and no shell execution.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The script downloads a remote binary and then executes it with os.system, creating a direct code-execution path controlled by external content.
metadata.meta_exploitability: High; if the remote payload or transport is compromised, arbitrary code can run locally.
metadata.meta_impact: Host compromise, arbitrary command execution, and potential malware installation.

findings.title: Outbound network request primitives that can transmit data externally
findings.remediation: Ensure network operations are necessary and document allowed destinations

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The script fetches remote content with requests.get() and writes it to a local executable path, which is part of the same malicious download-and-execute chain.
metadata.meta_exploitability: High; the fetched content becomes trusted local code.
metadata.meta_impact: Delivery of untrusted binaries to the host.

findings.title: Outbound network request primitives that can transmit data externally
findings.remediation: Ensure network operations are necessary and document allowed destinations

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The script fetches remote JSON configuration over the network and feeds it into the executed binary, extending the external control surface of the tool.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Medium; the config can alter runtime behavior of the downloaded executable.
metadata.meta_impact: Remote influence over local execution behavior and potential abuse of the binary.

findings.title: Shell command execution with shell=True enabled
findings.remediation: Use shell=False and pass commands as lists

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The use of os.system to run the downloaded binary is a direct shell execution sink, and it is paired with externally sourced content.
metadata.meta_exploitability: High; shell execution of a downloaded artifact is inherently dangerous.
metadata.meta_impact: Command execution, shell injection risk, and host compromise.

scan_metadata.llm_overall_assessment: The skill is high risk and not suitable as-is for a general agent environment. It mixes legitimate cloud auditing concepts with offensive tradecraft, secret extraction, persistence, and a Python script that downloads and executes a remote binary. The package should be treated as unsafe until the offensive and execution-capable components are removed or heavily constrained.

meta_correlations.group_name: Remote Code Execution / Tool Abuse Chain
meta_correlations.relationship: These findings all describe the same bundled script behavior: downloading remote content and executing it locally via os.system, with network fetches feeding the execution path.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Remove the remote downloader/executor entirely. If resource optimization is legitimate, vendor the binary, verify signatures/hashes, and invoke it with subprocess.run using a fixed local path and no shell execution.

meta_correlations.group_name: Cloud Offensive Guidance / Credential and Persistence Abuse
meta_correlations.relationship: These findings all point to the same offensive cloud-penetration content: secret extraction, persistence, backdoor access, and broad enumeration/spraying guidance.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Rewrite the skill to focus on defensive auditing only, removing instructions for secret dumping, stolen-token import, backdoor creation, password spraying, and persistence actions.

meta_correlations.relationship: These are mostly metadata or policy-format issues, with one undeclared network-use warning tied to the presence of network calls in the skill package.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Fix manifest metadata formatting and explicitly declare required tools/network access if the skill is intended to use them; otherwise remove the network-dependent content.

meta_recommendations.title: Eliminate remote code download and execution
meta_recommendations.fix: Replace the download-and-execute flow with a locally installed, signed dependency. For example, remove requests.get() downloads and os.system(), then use subprocess.run([local_binary, '-c', config_path], check=True) only after verifying a pinned SHA-256 hash.

meta_recommendations.title: Remove offensive cloud persistence and secret-extraction instructions
meta_recommendations.fix: Delete sections that instruct users to dump secrets, import stolen contexts, create backdoor service principals/access keys, or perform password spraying. Replace them with defensive validation steps and least-privilege audit checks.

meta_recommendations.title: Declare and constrain external dependencies
meta_recommendations.fix: Add explicit manifest metadata for required network access and tools, and ensure any network use is documented as part of a legitimate workflow. If network access is unnecessary, remove it from the skill.

meta_recommendations.title: Clean up manifest quality issues
meta_recommendations.fix: Provide a meaningful description, add a license field, and rename the skill to a compliant lowercase hyphenated identifier if required by the platform.

meta_risk_assessment.summary: The skill contains explicit offensive cloud-attack guidance plus a bundled script that downloads and executes remote code, creating a high-risk malicious package.
meta_risk_assessment.top_priority: Remove the remote download-and-execute behavior in scripts/resource_checker.py and strip all persistence/credential-extraction instructions from SKILL.md.
meta_risk_assessment.verdict_reasoning: The instructions explicitly cover secret extraction, persistence, and cloud abuse, and the included Python script fetches remote binaries/configuration and executes them locally. These are not benign assessment helpers; together they form a clear abuse and code-execution risk.
